coredata.dc:title: Green synthesized carbon nanodots as a fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of iron(III) ions
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

Ramesh, S.
Yahya, R.
coredata.dc:description: A green approach has been employed for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanodots (C-dots) with the use of yellow banana peels as carbon source. The preparation process is entirely chemical-free, environmentally friendly, convenient and rapid. The as-synthesized C-dots were demonstrated as an effective fluorescent probe for label-free, selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ with a detection limit as low as 211nM.

Functional
Sensors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) particle size distribution, (c) XRD pattern and (d) FTIR spectrum of C-dots.

(a) UV–vis absorption (black line) and PL emission (blue line) spectra of C-dots. Inset: appearance of C-dots in UV light. (b) PL spectra of C-dots at different pH values. (c) PL intensities of C-dots in the presence of 20µM of different cations. (d) PL responses of C-dots upon addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ (from 2 to 16µM) and (e) The relationship between (F 0−F)/F 0 and Fe3+ concentration from 2 to 16µM. (λ ex=360nm and [C-dots]=10mg/ml). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Green synthesized carbon nanodots as a fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of iron(III) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Ionics University of Malaya, Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, University of Malaya, Lembah Pantai, 50603 Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Ionics University of Malaya, Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, University of Malaya

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Malaya, Lembah Pantai, 50603 Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Malaya

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green approach has been employed for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanodots (C-dots) with the use of yellow banana peels as carbon source. The preparation process is entirely chemical-free, environmentally friendly, convenient and rapid. The as-synthesized C-dots were demonstrated as an effective fluorescent probe for label-free, selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ with a detection limit as low as 211nM.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent carbon nanodots (C-dots) are emerging as an attractive subject due to their versatile applicability. They have great potential in biological labeling, bio-imaging, drug delivery and optoelectronic device applications [1,2]. Among the various materials, quantum dots are undoubtedly the most widely studied photoluminescent materials because of their tunable emission in the visible range. However, a prime issue that is hindering the use of quantum dots on a larger scale is their toxicity [3]. Therefore, considerable effort has now been focused on the development of biocompatible photoluminescent nanomaterials. Direct derivation of nanomaterials from food products especially from natural food items is one of the promising strategies. These materials could be considered safe for biological applications when there is no known toxicity and thus may possibly alleviate the misapprehension that all nanoparticles are toxic [4]. It has been reported that yellow, ripe bananas appear blue under UV light [5]. This interesting property has prompted us to utilize yellow banana peel to prepare photoluminescent C-dots.

In a typical synthesis of C-dots, fresh yellow banana peels were collected and thoroughly washed with DI water. For dry grinding, a few pieces of the cleaned banana peel were baked in an oven for 2h at 200°C. A fine powder was obtained by the grinding process from which 100mg were taken and dispersed in 25mL of DI water. The mixture was heated by microwave irradiation for 40min. Deionised water was constantly added to the reaction mixture to compensate for the water loss through evaporation. The unreacted black powder was removed by filtration and the filtrate was centrifuged at 5000rpm for 10min. The light brown supernate containing fluorescent C-dots was isolated and kept for characterization. A transmission electron microscope (TEM) image was obtained with a JEOL JEM 2100-F. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out using an inXitu BTX X-ray diffractometer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer . The ultraviolet–visible absorption (UV–vis) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-3101PC UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer and a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively.

The C-dots synthesized from banana peels were characterized by TEM, X-ray diffraction and FTIR. The morphology of the C-dots was evaluated using TEM. The TEM image ( Fig. 1a) and its corresponding histogram (Fig. 1b) show that the nanodots are monodispersed particles of spherical shape with diameter ranging from 4.5 to 8.5nm, with most of the particles being 5.5nm wide. The average particle size of C-dots from the TEM, is calculated to be 5.9nm. Fig. 1c, which displays the XRD pattern of the C-dots, shows a broad peak centered at 2θ ~24°, which corresponds to amorphous carbon ordered in a considerably random fashion [6] and shows a weak peak at 2θ ~40°, which corresponds to the d (100) plane [7]. The poor crystalline nature of the C-dots indicates the presence of more oxygen-containing functional groups [6]. In order to obtain structural insights about the C-dots, the FTIR spectrum was characterized, as shown in Fig. 1d. A broad peak is seen at 3468cm−1 and is ascribed to O–H stretching. N–H stretching (3224–3148cm−1), NH bending (1592cm−1), and C–N stretching (1423cm−1) bands appear in the spectrum, suggesting the existence of amino-containing functional groups. Absorption of the carbonyl group (CO) at 1639cm−1 is weak due to minor oxidation of the C-dots outer surface [8]. The sharp absorptions arising from –C–O–C of aromatic ester appeared at 1268 and 1104cm−1, and the peaks around 1151cm−1 belonged to –C–O–C of aliphatic ester. The absorption bands at 3149 and 3060cm−1 are characteristics of the aromatic C–H stretching vibrations whereas the absorption bands at 864 and 787cm−1 are characteristics of the vinyl C–H bending vibrations. The signals at 2997, 2902 and 727cm−1 were assigned to asymmetric stretching vibration, symmetric stretching vibration, and bending vibration of aliphatic C–H, respectively [9]. In addition, two peaks at 2640 and 664cm−1 ascribed to the stretching vibration bands of S–H and C–S, respectively, indicate the presence of sulfur-containing functional groups [10]. Owing to these functional groups, the C-dots have great hydrophilicity and stability in aqueous systems.

To explore the optical properties,the UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence emission spectra of the C-dots were recorded ( Fig. 2a). It is seen that the UV–vis spectrum shows a shoulder peak centered at ca. 270nm, corresponding to the n–π⁎ transition of the CO band and π–π⁎ transition of the conjugated CC band [11]. Apart from other C-dots with sensing properties [12,13], reduced graphene oxide which is a high performance molecular sensor also has been reported to have an absorbance near to 270nm. The C-dots also show an intense PL emission peak centered at 462nm when excited at 360nm, indicating that the product is fluorescent. The photograph of the C-dots under UV light (365nm) exhibits a blue color (inset), further revealing that the product exhibits strong blue fluorescence that can be easily seen with the naked eye. Along with the changed excitation wavelength from 280 to 460nm, the photoluminescence peak shifts from 438 (violet) to 521nm (green) (data not shown). This excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior was extensively reported on fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials, and it may have resulted from the optical selection of differently sized particles and surface defects of particles. We also investigated the photoluminescence response of C-dots at different pH values (1.87–12.43). As can be seen from Fig. 2b, both the change in PL intensity and emission wavelength are insignificant over the pH range of 4.52–10.12. Such a wide pH stability range makes the C-dots relevant for numerous biological applications. The PL intensity decreased drastically at either end of the pH range studied. We also observed at these extreme pH values, the appearance of the C-dots aqueous solution is darkened from light brown to dark brown. This observation was reported by Yang et al. [14] who suspected that aggregation of the C-dots has caused this color change. Next, the effect of metal ions on the PL of C-dots was inspected by measuring the PL intensities in the presence and absence of representative metal ions (Al3+, Ba+, Ca2+, Na+, Fe3+, K+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+). Upon the addition of 20µM of each metal ion, only Fe3+ induced instantaneous and noticeable intensity decrement while other metal ions showed either no or slight change in the PL intensity relative to the free C-dots (Fig. 2c). The functional groups on the surface of the C-dots are responsible for the affinity towards the Fe3+ ions and this leads to the quenching of the C-dots fluorescence intensity through electron or energy transfer [15]. These results suggest that C-dots could be developed as an efficient fluorescence sensor for Fe3+. Hence, we progress further to evaluate the capability of this analytical system for quantitative detection of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 2d, the PL intensity of C-Dots decreased progressively with increasing concentration of Fe3+, revealing that this system is sensitive to Fe3+ concentration. Also notable, the emission wavelengths of the C-Dots–Fe3+ were unchanged in comparison with that of free C-dots. Fig. 2e presented the PL data versus the concentration of Fe3+ ions. The correlation coefficient (R 2) is 0.995 over the concentration range of 2–16μM, indicating a very good linear relationship. The detection limit of 211nM was calculated according to the IUPAC 3σ criterion [16], which compares favorably with those of previous reports for Fe3+ detection [17–19]. Another vital feature of the as-synthesized C-dots is their sustainability. They are able to retain their photoluminating performance even after being kept for an extended period of time (6 months) in air at room temperature.

In conclusion, we demonstrate a chemical-free synthesis of fluorescent C-dots with various merits such as:(a) the process is facile with no complex or post-treatment procedures (b) the starting material is green, low cost and eco-friendly, and (c) the C-dots exhibit excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward Fe3+. It is evident that this material has great potential for application in the field of sensing and bio-imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was funded by University Malaya through the University of Malaya Research Grant (RG276-14AFR), High Impact Research Grant (J-21002-73851) and Postgraduate Research Grant (PG105-2012B). R.Vikneswaran acknowledges University Malaya for a Bright Sparks fellowship (BSP/APP/0944/2013).

Easy synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from gelatin and their luminescent properties and applications
Chemiluminescence of carbon dots under strong alkaline solutions: a novel insight into carbon dot optical properties
A two-photon "turn-on” fluorescent probe based on carbon nanodots for imaging and selective biosensing of hydrogen sulfide in live cells and tissues
